value: 일본 국외 거주 외국인 여권 소지자. 'Temporary Visitor' 체류자격으로 입국한 자만 구매 가능. 일본 국적자 또는 일본 거주 외국인은 구매 불가.

value.비고: 2023년 10월 인상 후 유지. 2025-2026 추가 인상 없음.

value: 한국 국적자는 90일 이내 관광 목적 무비자 입국 가능. 여권 잔여 유효기간 제한 없음 (체류 기간 이상 권장). Visit Japan Web 사전 등록 권장.
Suica/PASMO 등 IC카드로 전철, 버스, 편의점 결제 가능. 물리 카드 재고 부족은 2025.3월 공식 해소. Welcome Suica(관광객용, 28일 유효) 주요 역에서 판매 재개. 모바일 Suica(Apple/Google) 및 Welcome Suica Mobile(iPhone, 180일 유효) 이용 가능.
지정석(Reserved)과 자유석(Non-reserved) 구분. JR Pass 소지 시 지정석 무료 예약 가능 (Nozomi/Mizuho 제외). 자유석은 선착순 탑승.
면세 범위: 주류 3병(760ml 이하), 담배 200개비(거주자)/400개비(비거주자), 향수 2oz, 기타 품목 20만엔 이하. 초과 시 신고 및 관세 부과.

value.비고: 구급차 무료, 치료비는 별도. 119/110은 일본어 우선이나 통역 연결 가능.

value: Visit Japan Web은 웹 기반 서비스(앱 불필요). 여권 정보, 입국심사(하선카드), 세관신고, 면세쇼핑 3개 항목 사전 등록. 등록 후 QR코드 발급 → 공항 도착 시 제시. 2026년 현재 필수는 아니나 강력 권장 (심사 시간 대폭 단축). 스크린샷 불가, 라이브 접속 필요.

신제도_2026_11월.방식: 환급형 (구매 시 세금 포함 → 출국 시 환급)

value: Only those who have foreign citizenship and whose status of residence is Temporary Visitor, Diplomat, or Official are eligible to use Visit Japan Web for duty-free purchases.

All foreign nationals who need to obtain a short-term visa to Japan and currently reside in Australia, Brazil, Cambodia, Canada, Saudi Arabia, South Africa, Taiwan, United Kingdom, or U.S.A. are eligible to apply for a single-entry tourism visa online.

Foreign travelers in Japan can access emergency services by dialing 110 for police and 119 for fire or medical emergencies; these numbers are toll-free and do not require coins when called from public phones.
Persons under 20 years of age must pay duties on all tobacco products brought into Japan.
Daily room rates at Tokyo hotels averaged 29,565 yen in 2024
Normal restaurants cost about $10-$15 per person; $20 can get larger or double portions

The Japan Rail Pass is only available to foreign tourists with a short-stay status or visa, indicated by a 'Temporary Visitor' stamp in the passport obtained through a manned ticket gate at the airport. Student visa or working holiday visa holders and Japanese citizens cannot buy or use the Japan Rail Pass unless they can prove they have foreign residency.
Suica cards are sold for the following prices: 1,000 yen, 2,000 yen, 3,000 yen, 4,000 yen, 5,000 yen and 10,000 yen. The price includes the card, a 500-yen deposit
Day tickets for international visitors are 8,000 yen ($56.07) per adult and 5,400 yen ($37.85) per child; for Japanese residents, 6,300 yen ($44.15) per adult and 4,500 yen ($31.54) per child
Adult ticket for local residents is 1,000 yen ($7.01); for visitors from outside the city, 2,500 yen ($17.52) starting March 2026
Starting July 2025, hikers on the four main routes to Mount Fuji summit must pay a fee of 4,000 yen (approx. $28 USD)
Many temples and shrines in Tokyo are free; some have small entrance fees of 300-500 yen ($2-4 USD) payable on the day of visit
Tokyo DisneySea 1-Day Passport costs approximately $60
teamLab Forest Fukuoka Immersive Art Experience Ticket costs approximately $27
New adult ticket price for non-residents is ¥2,500 (about $16), up from ¥1,000; local residents still pay original rate
Standard physical Suica cards expire if they are not used for 10 consecutive years; any use including riding trains or shopping resets the expiration period.

To use a Suica card, simply tap the card on the ticket gate at train stations and the fare will automatically be deducted from the card's balance.
In Japanese dining etiquette, it is customary to keep chopsticks neatly together and place them on the hashi-oki (chopstick rest) often provided at nicer restaurants when not in use.

The typical dinner time in Japan generally falls between 6:00 p.m. and 8:00 p.m., with families with young children often eating around 6:00 or 6:30 p.m., and adults with longer working hours sometimes eating as late as 8:00 or 9:00 p.m.

Travelers in Japan have three primary options for staying online: eSIMs, physical SIM cards, and pocket WiFi devices. Each option varies in pricing, coverage, ease of use, and data limits, and the best choice depends on phone compatibility, group size, and data needs.
Japan Travel SIM cards like the IIJmio Japan Travel SIM can be bought at BIC Camera shops throughout Tokyo, which have the best selection and can install the SIM card for ¥2200.
The cost of eating in Japan per day can range from 3,500 to 6,000 yen for budget travelers, up to 6,000 to 12,000 yen for mid-range travelers

Wi-Fi is not ubiquitous in Japan; it is less common and less easy to access compared to Europe or the Americas, so travelers should plan their connectivity options ahead of time.
Roaming with your local carrier costs ₹399–₹699 per day in India and $5–$10 per day in US/UK
Cash remains the most accepted payment method in Japan, especially in small restaurants, local shops, and rural areas.
Konbini payments are widely accepted in Japan, allowing customers to pay for online purchases, bills, or services in cash at over 56,000 convenience stores including 7-Eleven, Lawson, and FamilyMart.
In 2018, cash payments made up 82% of all payments in Japan.
Cash remains the most accepted payment method in Japan, and it is recommended to carry cash as a backup since some locations may not accept credit cards or digital payments.

You can add money to your IC card at almost all train stations in Japan at automatic ticket machines. To use the IC card, tap it on the ticket gate to enter and tap again when leaving the gate at your destination.

Japanese transportation is clean, quiet, and respectful. Following local etiquette makes the experience smoother for everyone.

Taking the train in Japan is the easiest and fastest way to travel between and within most major cities, with many cities offering day passes for unlimited travel on local subways, trains, trams, and buses.

The typical dinner time in Japan generally falls between 6:00 p.m. and 8:00 p.m., though this can vary depending on individual household routines and work or school schedules.

SIM cards can be purchased at arrival halls of major airports in Japan such as Narita, Haneda, and Kansai International Airport, where multiple service providers offer short-term data-only and voice-capable plans.

In Japan, reliable internet connectivity supports nearly every moment of the trip, improving safety, reducing stress, and saving time; therefore, first-time visitors are advised to prepare a dedicated connectivity solution such as renting pocket Wi-Fi, purchasing a SIM card, or using an eSIM for peace of mind.
To use a SIM card in Japan, travelers need an unlocked phone, then swap in the Japanese SIM card, turn on the phone, and enable mobile data.
In Japan, long lines outside food establishments often indicate a quality meal spot, especially if the line consists mostly of locals rather than tourists.
In Japanese dining etiquette, it is considered impolite to raise food above mouth level while eating.
In Japan, dinner is usually around 6 or 7 pm, but it can vary depending on the family and work schedules.

Travelers in Japan have three primary options for staying online: eSIMs, physical SIM cards, and pocket WiFi devices. The best choice depends on your phone compatibility, group size, travel style, and data needs.
For your first week in Japan, plan to have around ¥50,000 in cash for initial expenses such as airport transport, meals, and small purchases before needing to withdraw more from an ATM.
The AMAZFIT Balance 2 XT smartwatch can be connected to an iPhone by pairing it via Wi-Fi and completing the setup process, after which it is ready to use.

A phone that fails to connect in Japan is usually held back by its own settings rather than by local networks; confirming a few technical details before departure lays the groundwork for smooth, reliable mobile use once you arrive.
You can buy Japan Travel SIM cards like the IIJmio Japan Travel SIM at BIC Camera shops which are widespread in Tokyo and other cities.

To use mobile phone service in Japan, travelers should ensure their phone is unlocked and supports Japan's 4G LTE bands (Band 1, 3, 8, 18, 19, 21, and 28). Without an unlocked phone compatible with these bands, local SIM cards or eSIMs cannot be used.

IC travel cards can be used to make payments at many convenience stores, shops, and vending machines in Japan. Physical IC travel cards can be purchased at station ticket counters or automatic ticket vending machines at most railway stations. A deposit is required to buy an IC travel card.

IC travel cards and smartphone apps can be used to make payments at many convenience stores, shops, and vending machines in Japan. Physical IC travel cards can be purchased at station ticket counters or automatic ticket vending machines at most railway stations. A deposit is required to buy an IC travel card.
Japan remains a cash-heavy society with 70% of transactions still cash-based as of 2026.

IC cards such as Suica and Icoca are stored-value cards that can be recharged and are widely accepted for payment on trains, buses, many convenience stores, chain restaurants, vending machines, and coin lockers across Japan.
The Japan Rail Pass is valid for consecutive periods of 7, 14, or 21 days starting from the first day of use.

To save money, stay near metro stations in big cities and bus stops in smaller cities. Staying near a metro station is very important to easily access sightseeing spots and return before midnight when metro closes.
If the restaurant doesn’t take reservations and there are people waiting to be seated, such as at ramen or sushi shops, try not to linger after you’ve finished eating.
Museums typically open from 10:00 to 17:00 every day including weekends and national holidays
Each Suica is valid for 180 days from the issuance date (Welcome Suica Mobile)
Purchases can be made online via JR-EAST Train Reservation, at ticket offices in Japan, or at travel agencies outside of Japan.
Memorize or note the MRT stations you should ride and alight from when visiting attractions in Japan, as walking is key and you may encounter unique things outside of tourist areas.
Travelers can order a Japan SIM card online before arrival and pick it up at the airport upon arrival for easy activation and use.

Travelers should plan ahead for internet access in Japan because free Wi-Fi is limited, and mobile roaming tends to be expensive; using pocket Wi-Fi, SIM cards, or eSIMs is recommended for reliable connectivity during travel.

Yufuin is a rural hot spring town near Beppu, located in Kyushu, Japan.
Beppu is Japan's capital city of hot springs, located in Kyushu, Japan.
Fukuoka is Kyushu’s largest city and an international port connecting Japan to much of Asia.
The Welcome Suica card is an IC card specifically designed for short-term foreign visitors to Japan and must be booked online.
You can buy wifi dongles at the airport or many tourist shops in Japan, or order one from Amazon Japan for delivery to a convenience store nearby.

Sapporo Tourist Information Center (Sapporo Station) is open from 8:30 AM to 8:00 PM
Coaches arrive in Yufuin at about 10am and leave at about 5pm daily
Tipping is not common in Japan for services such as bars, cafes, restaurants, taxis, and hotels. It is generally not expected and can sometimes be refused politely.

Suica, Pasmo, Icoca and seven more of Japan's most popular IC cards were made compatible with each other in 2013, allowing travel on almost all trains, subways and buses in most of Japan's largest cities with a single card.
